questions.text: The compressibility factor Z=PV/RT for 1 mol of a real gas is greater than unity at a pressure of 1 atm and 273.15 K. The molar volume of the gas at STP will be :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The gas is less compressible than ideal gas. Hence, Vm>22.4L

The compressibility factor Z of a gas is defined as the ratio of the product of pressure P and volume V to the product of the number of moles n, universal gas constant R, and temperature T. Mathematically, it is expressed as: Z=PV/nRT At STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure), which is defined as 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm, the molar volume of an ideal gas is 22.4 L. If the compressibility factor of a gas is greater than unity at a pressure of 1 atm and 273.15 K, then the gas behaves less ideally.In such cases, the molar volume of the gas at STP will be greater than 22.4 L for an ideal gas.So in short we can say,the gas is less compressible than ideal gas. Hence, Vm>22.4L.

questions.text: 1 liter flask contains nitrogen along with a drop or two of water at 40° C, the total pressure being 760 Torr. If all the contents are transferred to another flask of 0.5L at the same temperature. The pressure of nitrogen setup in the second flask will be: (aqueous tension at 40 °C 55 torr):
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Not correct according to solution.

When a 1 liter flask containing nitrogen and a drop or two of water at 40°C is transferred to another flask of 0.5L at the same temperature, the pressure of nitrogen in the second flask can be calculated using Boyle’s law. Boyle’s law states that the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume at constant temperature. Initially, the total pressure in the 1 liter flask is 760 Torr. After transferring the contents to the 0.5L flask, the volume of the gas is halved. Therefore, according to Boyle’s law, the pressure of nitrogen in the second flask will be twice the original pressure, which is: P2 = P1 * (V1 / V2) = 760 * (1 / 0.5) = 1520 Torr However, we need to subtract the vapor pressure of water from this value to get the pressure of nitrogen alone. The vapor pressure of water at 40°C is 55 Torr. Therefore: P2 (dry gas) = P2 (wet gas) - aqueous tension = 1520 - 55 = 1465 Torr Therefore, the pressure of nitrogen setup in the second flask will be 1465 Torr. Note: aq. Tension is independent of volume, and depends on temperature.

questions.text: The compressibility factor of a gas is less than unity (1) at STP. Therefore,
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is incorrect for compressibility factor less than 1.

The compressibility factor (Z) of a gas is defined as the ratio of the actual volume (V) of the gas to the ideal gas volume (Vm) at the same temperature and pressure. Mathematically, Z is expressed as: Z = V / Vm At standard temperature and pressure (STP), the conditions are defined as a temperature of 273.15 K (0 degrees Celsius) and a pressure of 1 atmosphere (1 atm). For an ideal gas, the compressibility factor (Z) is exactly equal to 1(Z=1).This means that the actual volume (V) of the gas is equal to the ideal gas volume (Vm) at STP. Z=1 V=Vm However, if the compressibility factor (Z) is less than 1, it indicates that the actual volume (V) of the gas is smaller than the ideal gas volume (Vm) at STP. Since the ideal gas volume at STP is 22.4 liters (Vm = 22.4 L) for one mole of gas, if Z < 1, it implies that the actual volume (V) of the gas is smaller than 22.4 liters (Vm) at STP. Z<1 V<Vm attractive intermolecular forces dominates In other words, Vm < 22.4 liters, as the actual volume of the gas (V) is reduced due to attractive intermolecular forces or real gas behavior that deviates from the ideal gas behavior. This reduction in volume can occur for certain real gases under specific conditions, especially when the gas particles experience attractive forces that cause them to be closer together compared to an ideal gas.Factors such as molecular size, intermolecular interactions, and temperature can affect the compressibility factor and the deviation from ideal gas behavior.
Actual volume (V) of the gas is equal to the ideal gas volume (Vm) i.e 22.4 litres at STP when compressibility factor (Z) is equal to 1.
This is incorrect for compressibility factor less than 1.

options.explanation: The number of atoms per unit cell in a simple cubic, face-centered cubic and body-centred cubic unit cell are 1,4,2 respectively.(1) The number of atoms contained in a simple cubic unit cell of monoatomic substance is 1.8 atoms are present at 8 corners of a simple cubic unit cell. Each atom contributes one eigth to the unit cell. Total contribution = 8/8 ​= 1(2) The number of atoms contained in one face-centred cubic unit cell of monoatomic substance is 4.8 atoms are present at 8 corners of a face-centred cubic unit cell. questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: ABCABC is indeed not associated with an HCP (Hexagonal Close-Packed) structure. The HCP structure follows a different stacking sequence with ABAB, resulting in a hexagonal pattern when viewed from the top. In an HCP structure, there are two layers of atoms or ions in the unit cell, and the arrangement is hexagonal rather than cubic.

The sequence ABCABC is not a characteristic of a body-centered cubic (BCC) structure. The BCC structure has a different layering arrangement compared to the ABCABC sequence. In a BCC structure, the layers follow an AABBCC... sequence.

The sequence ABCABC typically does not describe a simple cubic (SC) structure. Simple cubic (SC) structure has a different arrangement where there is one atom at each corner of a cube, and the layers are not packed as densely as in an HCP structure. The SC structure is much simpler and has a lower packing efficiency compared to HCP and other closely packed structures.

In a CCP structure, the layers of atoms or ions are indeed stacked in a manner where each layer is positioned directly above the preceding layer, following the sequence ABCABC. This results in a cubic pattern when viewed from the top. This arrangement is commonly found in many metallic crystal structures, including those of elements like copper and aluminum.

questions.text: Which type of magnetic behaviour is shown by magnetite (Fe3O4)?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Magnetite (Fe3O4) does not exhibit ferromagnetism because its magnetic moments are not all perfectly aligned in the same direction, which is a key characteristic of ferromagnetic materials.

Magnetite (Fe3O4) exhibits ferrimagnetic behavior. Ferrimagnetism arises due to the unequal magnetic moments in opposite direction, resulting net magnetic moment in one direction. Magnetic moments of the domains are aligned in parallel and anti-parallel directions in unequal numbers. These materials are weakly attracted by magnetic fields as compared to ferromagnetic substances. On heating, substance loses its ferrimagnetism property and becomes paramagnetic. Fe3O4, ferrites like MgFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4 are examples of ferrimagnetism.

Magnetite (Fe3O4) does not exhibit antiferromagnetism because of its particular crystal structure and the arrangement of its magnetic moments. In antiferromagnetic materials, adjacent magnetic moments align in opposite directions, leading to a net magnetic moment of zero. This results in the cancellation of magnetic effects at the macroscopic level.

Magnetite (Fe3O4) does not exhibit diamagnetism because it contains magnetic ions (Fe2+ and Fe3+) that have unpaired electrons. Diamagnetism is a property of materials in which all of the electrons are paired, resulting in no net magnetic moment.

questions.text: The crystal system having a = β ≠ c and α = β = γ = 90° is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The hexagonal system has four crystallographic axes consisting of three equal horizontal, or equilateral axes at 120 degrees to each other, as well as one vertical axis which is perpendicular to the other three. This vertical axis can be longer or shorter than the horizontal axes.

In the Tetragonal crystal system: The three crystallographic axes are all perpendicular to each other (α = β = γ = 90°). Two of the axes (a and b) have the same length (a = b), and these are perpendicular to each other. The third axis (c) has a different length (c ≠ a or b). So, in the Tetragonal crystal system, the unit cell is a rectangular prism with two edges of equal length (a and b) and one edge of a different length (c), with all right angles between the edges.

The Monoclinic crystal system is one of the seven crystal systems in crystallography. In the Monoclinic system, the crystal structure is characterized by the following parameters: The three crystallographic axes are all of different lengths. Two of the axes (a and c) are perpendicular to each other, with α = γ = 90 degrees, meaning they form right angles. The third axis (b) has a different length from a and c, and it is not perpendicular to either of them. The angle between the a and b axes, denoted as β, is not 90 degrees. β ≠ 90 degrees.

In the Triclinic system, all three crystallographic axes have different lengths, and all three interaxial angles are different, meaning α ≠ β ≠ γ ≠ 90°. The Triclinic system has the least symmetry among the seven standard crystal systems.

questions.text: The product of PV of a gas has unit of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The product of pressure (P) and volume (V) of a gas does not have the unit of force.The unit of force is typically expressed in Newtons (N) or pound-force (lbf),and it is not related to the product of pressure and volume.

The product of pressure and volume (PV) of a gas does not have the unit of entropy.Entropy is a thermodynamic property that relates to the amount of energy in a system that cannot be used to do work.Its unit is joules per kelvin (J/K) in the International System of Units(SI).

The product of pressure and volume (PV) of a gas has the same unit as that of work.Work is defined as the energy transfer that occurs when a force acts on an object and causes it to move a certain distance.The unit of work is the joule(J) in the International System of Units(SI).

The product of pressure and volume(PV) of a gas does not have the unit of enthalpy.Enthalpy is another thermodynamic property that relates to the total heat content of a system at constant pressure.Its unit is also joules (J) in SI.

questions.explanation: The product of pressure and volume (PV) of a gas has the unit of work (joules).When a gas undergoes a change in volume against an external pressure,work is done.The work done by the gas is given by the formula W=P*ΔV,where P is the pressure and ΔV is the change in volume.

questions.text: Under what condition of temperature and pressure will a real gas behave most like an ideal gas:

options.explanation: At low temperatures and low pressures,real gases tend to behave more like ideal gases.At these conditions,the gas molecules have lower kinetic energy,and their interactions with each other are less significant,leading to less deviation from ideal gas behavior.

The standard temperature and standard pressure(STP) conditions are defined as 0 degrees Celsius(273.15 K) and 1 atmosphere(101.325 kPa or 1 bar). At STP,real gases do not necessarily behave like ideal gases.In fact,at these conditions,most real gases exhibit noticeable deviations from ideal gas behavior.

At low temperatures and high pressures,real gases are more likely to deviate significantly from ideal gas behavior.Under these conditions,the attractive forces between gas molecules become more pronounced,causing larger deviations.

At high temperatures and low pressures,real gases behave more like ideal gases.As the temperature increases,gas molecules gain more kinetic energy and move more freely,leading to reduced intermolecular interactions and closer approximation to ideal gas behavior.

questions.explanation: Real gases behave most like ideal gases under conditions of high temperature and low pressure.When the temperature is high,the kinetic energy of gas molecules is large enough to overcome intermolecular forces,and at low pressure,the gas molecules are far apart,reducing the influence of attractive forces.As a result,the deviations from ideal gas behavior are minimized,and the gas approximates the behavior of an ideal gas.

The molecules in the bulk of the liquid are too tightly packed together to escape into the air.This is why the rate of evaporation is affected by surface area. The opposite of evaporation is condensation, which is the process of a gas changing into a liquid. Condensation is an exothermic process, which means that it releases heat. When a gas condenses, the molecules lose heat and then return to a liquid state.

questions.text: Which has maximum volatility?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Miscellaneous

options.explanation: Diethyl ether has the maximum volatility. Volatility is a measure of how easily a substance changes from a liquid to a gas. It is affected by the strength of the intermolecular forces between the molecules in the substance. Diethyl ether has the weakest intermolecular forces of the three substances. This is because it is a nonpolar molecule, and nonpolar molecules do not have any permanent dipoles. Without permanent dipoles, they cannot form hydrogen bonds or dipole-dipole interactions. The only intermolecular forces that exist between diethyl ether molecules are London dispersion forces.

Ethanol and water, on the other hand, are polar molecules. This means that they have permanent dipoles. Polar molecules can form hydrogen bonds and dipole-dipole interactions. These intermolecular forces are stronger than London dispersion forces, so ethanol and water have higher boiling points than diethyl ether.
Water being a polar molecule exhibits strong Hydrogen Bonding(H-bonding).So this strong imermolecular forces accounts for low volatility rate.

Polar molecules have a positive end and a negative end. The positive end of one molecule is attracted to the negative end of another molecule. This creates a dipole-dipole interaction that is weaker than hydrogen bonding.So dipole forces cannot exist between zero group elements.

The most important type of intermolecular force for noble gases is London dispersion forces(LDF). London dispersion forces are caused by the temporary uneven distribution of electrons in a molecule. This creates a temporary dipole, which can induce a dipole in another molecule. The attraction between these induced dipoles is a London dispersion force.The increase in size and molecular weight increases the intermolecular forces down the group and results in an increase in boiling point. This is because it takes more energy to break the intermolecular forces and turn the liquid into a gas.

Debye forces are caused by the temporary uneven distribution of electrons in a molecule. This creates a temporary dipole, which can induce a dipole in another molecule. The attraction between these induced dipoles is a Debye force.
